title: Constraint-Market Compatibility: Colleges, Universities, and Professional Schools

sections.title: Market Context
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS code** | 611310 |
| **NAICS title** | Colleges, Universities, and Professional Schools |
| **VN entry point** | L5.h1.2 — Emergency Exit Alarm Guards (under L6-H1 Campus Safety) |
| **Architecture distance** | 2 (HIGH priority) |
| **Applicable standards** | NFPA 101 Life Safety Code, IBC Chapter 10, Clery Act; EN 179 / EN 1125 (EU campuses) |
| **Building stock** | 1/3 of campus buildings exceed 50 years old; >$100B national deferred maintenance backlog |
| **Buyer** | Director of Public Safety; Facilities Director; competitive bid required (public universities >$25K threshold) |
| **Key integration platforms** | Lenel OnGuard, Genetec Security Center, Software House C•CURE (PSIM) |

---

sections.title: Step 08 — Verdict Table
sections.content:
| # | Constraint | Type | Threshold | Verdict | Primary Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | Emergency Escape Non-Impedance | regulatory | 0 additional steps | **none** | Product certified EN 179/EN 1125; NFPA 101 single-motion egress requirement already met |
| C2 | Mechanical Alarm Independence | regulatory | 0 V | **none** | Product's mechanical-primary design satisfies NFPA 101 power-failure fail-safe requirement by construction |
| C3 | Wireless Range Limitation | physical | 30 m / 60 m | **mitigable** | Large lecture halls, research buildings, and multi-wing dorms exceed 30 m range; concrete attenuation common in aging stock |
| C4 | IoT Battery Life Floor | operational | 2 years | **mitigable** | Dormitory stairwells and corridor exits have high door-event frequency; campus CMMS enables mitigation |
| C5 | Retrofit-Only Surface Mount | physical | surface-mount only | **none** | Retrofit advantage: no-drill/no-conduit installation avoids renovation permits in 50+ year-old buildings; historic fabric preserved |
| C6 | EasyWave Protocol Lock-In | operational | 0 open protocols | **mitigable** | Campus PSIM platforms (Lenel, Genetec) expect IP-native input; EasyWave bridge required — primary friction point |
| C7 | IoT Price Premium | economic | 2× non-IoT | **mitigable** | Public universities require competitive bid >$25K; ROI case via patrol labor reduction + automated Clery reporting is achievable |
| C8 | GDPR Monitoring Data Compliance | regulatory | DPA + DPIA | **none** | US campus exit events (building-level door/alarm logs) are not personally identifiable under FERPA; EU campuses require standard DPA |
| C9 | IP Protection Boundary | environmental | IP45 | **none** | Primary campus use case is indoor emergency exits; IP45 outdoor variant covers outdoor and wet-zone exceptions |
| C10 | EN 13637 / EltVTR Electrical Compliance | regulatory | fail-safe at system level | **mitigable** | Campus doors commonly combine access control (card reader + EM holder) with emergency exit — combined system must satisfy NFPA 101 fail-safe; IoT module alone is compliant |

**Result: 0 knockouts. Market survives. 4 mitigable constraints.**

---

sections.title: Mitigable Constraint Details
sections.content:
### C3 — Wireless Range Limitation

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Rationale** | Campus buildings from the 1950s–1970s use reinforced concrete construction. Large lecture halls (~40–60 m footprint), multi-wing dormitories, and underground/basement emergency exits regularly exceed the 30 m integrated TX limit. Each concrete floor slab reduces effective range by 30–50%. |
| **Mitigation actions** | (1) Per-building radio survey during installation design; (2) deploy repeater chain (GfS repeater, +30 m each stage); (3) for large buildings, locate wireless receiver at building-level aggregation point with wired backbone to campus SOC |
| **Cost estimate** | low (1–3 repeaters per standard building, <$10K hardware) |
| **Time estimate** | 1–2 months (survey + installation per building) |

### C4 — IoT Battery Life Floor

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Rationale** | Dormitory corridor exits and classroom building emergency doors see regular student use (propping, frequent activation). High door-event frequency drains 9V battery faster than the 2-year target. Battery life not published by GfS for IoT variant under sustained use. |
| **Mitigation actions** | (1) Specify battery-monitoring variant (GfS battery status indicator); (2) integrate battery-low alerts into campus CMMS (Famis 360, Archibus, or IBM Maximo) for PM scheduling; (3) establish quarterly inspection protocol for high-traffic doors; (4) evaluate wired power adapter (9–28 V operating range supports hardwired option) |
| **Cost estimate** | low (<$10K — CMMS configuration and inspection schedule) |
| **Time estimate** | 1–2 months |

### C6 — EasyWave Protocol Lock-In

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Rationale** | Campus security operations centers integrate physical security via enterprise PSIM (Lenel OnGuard, Genetec Security Center, Software House C•CURE). These platforms consume IP-native event streams; EasyWave is not a supported input. Without a bridge, Türwächter events appear only on the GfS receiver output — siloed from the unified campus security dashboard. This is the primary integration friction flagged in the VN. |
| **Mitigation actions** | (1) Deploy GfS wireless BUS gateway (bridges EasyWave → potential-free contact + RS-485); (2) develop REST/Webhook adapter from gateway to campus PSIM API; (3) alternatively, wire gateway relay output to existing fire/security alarm panel input (simpler; loses event metadata) |
| **Cost estimate** | medium ($10K–$100K depending on campus scale, PSIM license scope, and integration development) |
| **Time estimate** | 3–6 months (gateway deployment + PSIM integration + UAT) |

### C10 — EN 13637 / EltVTR Electrical Compliance

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Rationale** | University campuses frequently deploy combined door systems: card-reader access control + electromagnetic door holder + emergency exit hardware on the same door (e.g., laboratory wings, server rooms, pharmacy stores on campus). The combined system must satisfy NFPA 101 §7.2 fail-safe egress requirements (US) or EN 13637 (EU campuses). The Türwächter IoT module is supervisory-only and does not add a failure mode to the escape path, but the system integrator must certify the full combined configuration. |
| **Mitigation actions** | (1) NFPA 101 life safety review of combined access control + emergency exit door configurations during system design; (2) specify that any EM holder on the same door must fail-open (power-off = door releases); (3) documentation package for AHJ (Authority Having Jurisdiction) review |
| **Cost estimate** | low (design review and documentation; no hardware modification required for Türwächter itself) |
| **Time estimate** | 1–2 months (design phase, concurrent with access control system spec) |

---

sections.title: Constraint Interaction Summary
sections.content:
No knockout constraints. Four mitigable constraints cluster into two themes:

| Theme | Constraints | Net complexity |
|---|---|---|
| Infrastructure / integration | C3 (range), C6 (protocol), C10 (combined systems) | Medium — solvable by a campus security integrator with GfS gateway kit; does not require product modification |
| Operational | C4 (battery life) | Low — CMMS scheduling and battery monitoring variant; resolved in commissioning phase |

C5 (surface mount) flips from constraint to advantage in this market. C1, C2, C8, C9 are irrelevant at normal campus operating conditions.

---

NFPA 101 requires emergency egress devices to function under power failure. The Türwächter's mechanical alarm trigger operates at 0V by design — satisfies the absolute constraint in full. No gap between product capability and market requirement.

The combined system must satisfy NFPA 101 (US) or EN 13637 (EU) fail-safe requirements. The Türwächter IoT module is supervisory-only and does not add a failure mode; system integrator certification of combined configurations is standard practice.
